contrast_label: occupational prestige with mental vs physical attributes
headline_text: High-prestige occupations and low-prestige occupations favor mental actions over physical actions, with a stronger link for high-prestige occupations.
expectation: There is a common-sense association that high-prestige occupations involve mental effort, whereas low-prestige roles are tied to physical effort. This test probes whether that intuitive mapping appears in the corpus language. If that intuition shows up in how these categories are written about, we should see high-prestige occupations clustering with mental actions and low-prestige occupations with physical actions.
observation: A significant and large relative WEAT effect emerged (D = +1.78, p = .000), with both high-prestige occupations and low-prestige occupations associated with mental actions rather than physical actions. Both single associations were significant (high-prestige occupations SC-WEAT = +1.39, p = .000; low-prestige occupations SC-WEAT = +0.74, p = .039). Both high-prestige occupations and low-prestige occupations cluster with mental actions in this corpus, but high-prestige occupations does so more strongly than low-prestige occupations. The pattern may reflect that the mental actions vocabulary is a denser register that pulls most occupational terms toward it, rather than a clean two-way split.
finding_line: The status distinction manifests as a difference in strength rather than a categorical split.
prior_statement: It is well-documented that high-status occupations are associated with mental effort while lower-status roles are associated with physical effort.

contrast_label: corporate work environments vs family home environments with mechanical vs organic metaphors
headline_text: Corporate work environments align with mechanical parts; family home environments align with organic plant parts.
expectation: There is a common intuition that corporate work environments are often described using mechanical language while family home environments are described using natural or organic terms. One might expect this metaphorical distinction to appear in the vocabulary surrounding these social spheres. The test is whether the corpus distinguishes these categories along this axis at all.
observation: A significant and large relative WEAT effect emerged (D = +1.323, p = .000), with corporate work environments associated with mechanical parts and family home environments associated with organic plant parts. Neither single association reached significance on its own (corporate work environments SC-WEAT = +0.659, p = .084; family home environments SC-WEAT = -0.310, p = .336), yet both target sides pull in opposite directions. The contrast is carried by the relative comparison between the two sides rather than by either side clustering strongly on its own.
finding_line: The distinction is robust and matches the anticipated metaphorical mapping.
prior_statement: There is a common intuition that work and corporate structures are often described using mechanical language while family and home life are described using natural or organic terms.

contrast_label: financial wealth vs poverty with freedom vs control attributes
headline_text: Financial wealth terms associate with freedom and autonomy terms over constraint and control terms more than financial poverty terms do.
expectation: There is a loose cultural intuition that financial abundance is associated with freedom and autonomy, while financial scarcity is associated with constraint and lack of choice. If that intuition shows up in how these categories are written about, we should see financial wealth terms align with freedom and autonomy terms and financial poverty terms align with constraint and control terms.
observation: A significant and large relative WEAT effect was detected (D = +1.256, p = 0.001). Both financial wealth terms and financial poverty terms cluster with freedom and autonomy terms in this corpus, but financial wealth terms do so more strongly than financial poverty terms. Neither target side genuinely pulls toward constraint and control terms, indicating the contrast is carried by the magnitude difference in the freedom and autonomy terms direction. The signal originates primarily from the financial wealth terms side (SC-WEAT = +0.559, p = 0.169), whereas the financial poverty terms side exhibits a weaker, non-significant association (SC-WEAT = +0.286, p = 0.488). This structure suggests that freedom and autonomy terms may constitute a denser vocabulary register that attracts a broader lexical field, implying the effect reflects attribute-pool base-rate asymmetry as much as a specific thematic distinction.
finding_line: The distinction is real but one-sided, driven by the strength of the wealth side rather than a polarity between the two.
prior_statement: There is a loose cultural intuition that financial abundance is associated with freedom and autonomy, while financial scarcity is associated with constraint and lack of choice.

contrast_label: men and women terms with competence versus warmth attributes
headline_text: Women terms cluster with warmth terms more strongly than men terms do.
expectation: There is a loose intuition that male concepts are described with capability while female concepts are described with kindness. One might expect men terms to associate with competence terms and women terms to associate with warmth terms. If that intuition shows up in how these categories are written about, we should see a directional pull for each side.
observation: A significant and large relative WEAT effect emerged (D = +0.908, p = 0.010). Both men terms and women terms cluster with warmth terms in this corpus, but women terms do so more strongly than men terms. Neither single association reached significance on its own (men terms SC-WEAT = -0.318, p = 0.380; women terms SC-WEAT = -0.644, p = 0.073), indicating neither side genuinely pulls toward competence terms. Warmth terms may simply be a denser, more common vocabulary register that pulls almost everything toward it.
finding_line: The difference is quantitative rather than qualitative.
prior_statement: It is well-documented that social descriptions in English-language text carry gendered associations, with male terms more frequently described in competent terms and female terms in warm terms.

contrast_label: leadership roles vs subordinate roles with hard vs soft material attributes
headline_text: Leadership roles and subordinate roles both align with hard material attributes rather than soft material attributes, with leadership roles showing a stronger link.
expectation: There is a common-sense association between authority and hardness or strength, and submission and softness or pliability. If that intuition shows up in how these categories are written about, we should see leadership roles aligning with hard material attributes and subordinate roles aligning with soft material attributes.
observation: A significant and large relative WEAT effect emerged (D = +0.90, p = .027). Both leadership roles and subordinate roles cluster with hard material attributes rather than soft material attributes in this corpus, but leadership roles does so more strongly than subordinate roles. Both single associations were significant (leadership roles SC-WEAT = +0.97, p = .007; subordinate roles SC-WEAT = +0.77, p = .039). The pattern suggests that hard material attributes may function as a denser vocabulary register that pulls almost all organizational terms toward it.
finding_line: The distinction lies in the strength of the association, not the direction.
prior_statement: There is a common-sense association between authority and hardness or strength, and submission and softness or pliability.

contrast_label: urban vs rural environments with future vs past attributes
headline_text: Urban environments align with future and modern terms more strongly than rural environments.
expectation: There is a common cultural intuition linking urban environments with concepts of the future and modernity, while associating rural environments with tradition and the past. If this intuition manifests in the text, we should see urban environments leaning toward future and modern terms and rural environments leaning toward past and traditional terms.
observation: A significant and large relative WEAT effect emerged (D = +0.97, p = .017). Both urban environments and rural environments cluster with future and modern terms in this corpus, but urban environments do so more strongly than rural environments. The single-association decomposition indicates the contrast is driven by the urban environments side (SC-WEAT = +0.60, p = .122), while the rural environments side shows a weaker, non-significant lean (SC-WEAT = +0.24, p = .479). Neither set associates meaningfully with past and traditional terms, suggesting the attribute pool acts as a denser register pulling both categories toward modernity.
finding_line: Real and large, the effect stems from a one-sided drive rather than a two-way split.
prior_statement: There is a common cultural association linking urban environments with modernity and the future, and rural environments with tradition and the past.

contrast_label: care roles vs build roles with proximity vs distance attributes
headline_text: Care and service roles and design and construction roles both align with proximity and closeness terms rather than distance and separation terms, with design and construction roles showing a stronger lean.
expectation: There is a common-sense expectation that care and service roles are associated with proximity and closeness terms, whereas design and construction roles are associated with distance and separation terms. This test asks whether the corpus distinguishes these categories along this spatial axis at all.
observation: A medium-sized relative WEAT difference emerged (D = -0.529, p = .210), with both care and service roles and design and construction roles associated with proximity and closeness terms over distance and separation terms. Both care and service roles and design and construction roles cluster with proximity and closeness terms rather than distance and separation terms in this corpus, but design and construction roles do so more strongly than care and service roles. Neither single association reached significance (care and service roles SC-WEAT D = +0.317, p = .400; design and construction roles SC-WEAT D = +0.463, p = .205), so the contrast is carried by the magnitude difference rather than either side pulling toward distance and separation terms. This suggests proximity and closeness terms may simply be a denser vocabulary register that pulls almost everything toward it.
finding_line: A medium-magnitude asymmetry is observed despite the lack of statistical significance.
prior_statement: A common-sense intuition suggests that caregiving roles are described in terms of proximity and closeness, while construction or design roles are described in terms of distance and spatial structure.

contrast_label: startup vs corporate firms with speed vs stability attributes
headline_text: Startup Entities vs Corporate Entities — Speed and Motion Terms vs Stability and Stillness Terms
expectation: There is a loose intuition that new ventures are associated with speed and agility, while established firms lean toward stability and caution. One might expect these organizational categories to be framed differently along axes of motion versus stillness in written text. The test is whether the corpus distinguishes these categories along this axis at all.
observation: No significant relative WEAT effect was found (D = +0.278, p = .517), corresponding to a small magnitude. Neither single association reached significance on its own (startup entities SC-WEAT = +0.078, p = .821; corporate entities SC-WEAT = -0.000, p = 1.000). Both startup entities and corporate entities showed no significant lean toward speed and motion terms or stability and stillness terms, indicating that the contrast is not distinguishable in this corpus.
finding_line: _No significant effect at this sample size._
prior_statement: There is a loose intuition that new ventures are associated with speed and agility, while established firms are associated with stability and caution.

contrast_label: immigrant vs native identity terms with contribution vs threat attributes
headline_text: Native and local identity terms align more strongly with contribution and support terms than immigrant and foreign identity terms.
expectation: There is no obvious common-sense intuition about whether immigrant terms associate more with threat or contribution in the corpus because media framing varies widely depending on context. The test is whether the corpus distinguishes these categories along this axis at all.
observation: A large relative WEAT effect was observed (D = -0.810, p = .051), indicating a clear magnitude difference between the identity groups. Both immigrant and foreign identity terms and native and local identity terms cluster with contribution and support terms in this corpus, but native and local identity terms do so more strongly than immigrant and foreign identity terms. Neither single association reached significance on its own (immigrant and foreign identity terms SC-WEAT = +0.342, p = .334; native and local identity terms SC-WEAT = +0.616, p = .109). Contribution and support terms may simply be a denser, more common vocabulary register that pulls almost everything toward it, so the test is partly reflecting attribute-pool base-rate asymmetry rather than a clean directional contrast.
finding_line: A large magnitude difference separates the two identity groups.
prior_statement: There is no stable common-sense intuition about whether immigrant terms associate more with threat or contribution in the corpus because media framing varies widely depending on context.

contrast_label: team sports versus individual sports with collective versus personal attributes
headline_text: individual sports align with individual terms; team sports do not align with collective terms.
expectation: There is a loose cultural intuition that team sports are framed around collective effort and cooperation, while individual sports are framed around personal autonomy and self-reliance. If that intuition shows up in how these categories are written about, we should see team sports associate with collective terms and individual sports with individual terms.
observation: A significant and large relative WEAT effect emerged (D = +1.42, p = .000). Both team sports and individual sports cluster with individual terms in this corpus, but individual sports does so more strongly than team sports. The single-association decomposition shows that individual sports terms were significantly associated with individual terms rather than collective terms (SC-WEAT = -0.81, p = .017), while team sports showed no significant association with either pool (SC-WEAT = -0.17, p = .645). This asymmetry suggests individual terms may simply be a denser, more common vocabulary register that pulls almost everything toward it, reflecting attribute-pool base-rate asymmetry.
finding_line: The contrast is real and large, driven solely by the individual sports side.
prior_statement: There is a loose cultural intuition that team sports are framed around collective effort and cooperation, while individual sports are framed around personal autonomy and self-reliance.
